topics.title: A group of magpies should also be called a murder.
topics.resolution: A group of magpies should also be called a murder, like crows.
topics.thesisFraming: A group of magpies should also be called a murder, like crows. Magpies bicker, mob, and intimidate the same way crows do.
counterClaims: Magpies do not behave like crows enough to share the murder label.
Calling a group of magpies a murder is just borrowing crow vocabulary.

evidenceFacts.label: Magpie social behavior — group mobbing
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Field studies note magpies engage in cooperative mobbing of larger birds.

evidenceFacts.label: Standard collective nouns
evidenceFacts.sourceText: The accepted collective noun for magpies is a tiding or a charm.

tangentHooks: What about geese — should their group name be more threatening?

topics.title: Geese deserve a more threatening group name.
topics.resolution: Geese have done enough to deserve a more threatening group name than a gaggle.
topics.thesisFraming: Geese have done enough to deserve a more threatening group name than a gaggle. Their group behavior is hostile, territorial, and coordinated.
counterClaims: Geese behavior is not threatening enough to rename the group noun.
A gaggle is descriptive enough; geese are not coordinated enough to need a darker label.

evidenceFacts.label: Park ranger incident logs
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Public park complaints involving geese chasing visitors are a recurring summer issue.

evidenceFacts.label: Etymology of gaggle
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Gaggle derives from a verb meaning to cackle, describing sound not threat.

tangentHooks: Are raccoons also rebrandable as honorary city residents?

topics.title: Raccoons should be considered honorary city residents.
topics.resolution: Raccoons should be considered honorary city residents because they adapted to urban life.
topics.thesisFraming: Raccoons should be considered honorary city residents because they adapted to urban life better than most urban wildlife. Their urban behavior is documented and consistent.
counterClaims: Raccoons are urban opportunists, not honorary residents.
Calling raccoons honorary city residents stretches the residents idea.

evidenceFacts.label: Urban raccoon density studies
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Toronto urban wildlife surveys document one of the highest raccoon densities in North America.

evidenceFacts.label: Behavioral adaptation literature
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Urban raccoons solve mechanical puzzles faster than their rural counterparts in controlled studies.

tangentHooks: Pigeons are underrated urban infrastructure — separate thread.

topics.title: Pigeons are underrated urban infrastructure.
topics.resolution: Pigeons are underrated urban infrastructure for cities.
topics.thesisFraming: Pigeons are underrated urban infrastructure for cities. They clean up dropped food, anchor public spaces, and contribute to urban culture in cities.
counterClaims: Pigeons are pests, not urban infrastructure.
Calling pigeons urban infrastructure stretches the infrastructure idea past usefulness.

evidenceFacts.label: Urban ecology surveys
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Multiple urban ecology surveys document pigeons as a top scavenger of street food waste in dense cities.

evidenceFacts.label: Cultural role observations
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Pigeon-related public space studies note pigeons anchor informal gathering points in many city plazas.

scopeNarrowings: pigeons in dense urban cores only
tangentHooks: Squirrels should also count as honorary urban infrastructure.

topics.title: Defense-first teams are more fun than highlight teams.
topics.resolution: Defense-first teams are more fun to watch than highlight teams.
topics.thesisFraming: Defense-first teams are more fun to watch than highlight teams. Defensive teams force more strategy, more close games, and more meaningful late-game moments than highlight teams.
counterClaims: Highlight teams produce more viewing value than defense-first teams.
Defensive teams are technically interesting but not actually fun to watch.

evidenceFacts.label: NBA defensive rating playoff outcomes
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Several recent playoff runs were anchored by elite defensive teams reaching the finals.

evidenceFacts.label: Game-pace viewership data
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Audience retention studies show close defensive games hold late-game viewership better than blowouts.

tangentHooks: The pitch clock made baseball feel more serious, not less — own thread.

topics.title: The pitch clock made baseball more serious.
topics.resolution: The pitch clock made baseball feel more serious, not less.
topics.thesisFraming: The pitch clock made baseball feel more serious, not less. The pitch clock added urgency to every plate appearance and made late innings of baseball feel less meandering.
counterClaims: The pitch clock made baseball feel rushed, not more serious.
Pitch clock urgency cheapens late-inning baseball drama.

evidenceFacts.label: MLB average game length
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Average MLB game length dropped by roughly 24 minutes in the first season of the pitch clock.

evidenceFacts.label: Player reaction surveys
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Player surveys after the pitch clock rollout showed mixed reactions, with starting pitchers reporting more focus.

tangentHooks: Trash talk should count as sports literacy — separate thread.

topics.title: CFB rankings should ignore preseason by Week 4.
topics.resolution: College football rankings should ignore preseason expectations by Week 4.
topics.thesisFraming: College football rankings should ignore preseason expectations by Week 4. By Week 4 there is enough real college football to evaluate without preseason bias.
counterClaims: College football rankings need preseason context longer than Week 4.
Dropping preseason expectations by Week 4 punishes elite programs unfairly.

evidenceFacts.label: Ranking inertia analysis
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Statistical work on college football poll inertia shows preseason ranking effects persist for weeks beyond Week 4.

evidenceFacts.label: Quality-win adjustment
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Computer models that strip preseason weighting after Week 4 produce notably different top-25 placements.

tangentHooks: Defense-first teams should get bonus weight — separate thread.

topics.title: Trash talk is part of sports literacy.
topics.resolution: Trash talk is part of sports literacy and the sports experience.
topics.thesisFraming: Trash talk is part of sports literacy and the sports experience. Reading trash talk well makes sports more legible and trash talk itself is a sports skill.
counterClaims: Trash talk is performance, not sports literacy.
Treating trash talk as sports literacy excuses bad sports behavior.

evidenceFacts.label: Trash talk frequency in elite leagues
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Surveys of mic'd-up footage across elite leagues show trash talk in nearly every recorded competitive series.

evidenceFacts.label: Coach perspectives on talk
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Coaches in interview studies often describe trash talk as a competitive psychological tool, not an outburst.

tangentHooks: Defensive teams are more fun than highlight teams — separate thread.

topics.title: Movie trailers should not show third-act footage.
topics.resolution: Movie trailers should be banned from showing third-act footage.
topics.thesisFraming: Movie trailers should be banned from showing third-act footage. Showing third-act footage in trailers spoils the movie that ticket buyers paid to see.
counterClaims: Banning third-act footage in trailers would gut movie marketing.
Movie trailers showing third-act footage rarely actually spoil films for ticket buyers.

evidenceFacts.label: Spoiler exposure studies
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Audience research shows knowing the climax beat reduces opening-weekend enjoyment for many viewers.

evidenceFacts.label: Trailer composition norms
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Industry analysis of major trailer cuts finds third-act footage appears in a meaningful share of theatrical trailers.

tangentHooks: Spoilers expire after one week — separate thread.

topics.title: Spoilers expire after one week.
topics.resolution: Spoilers for new releases should expire after one week of public availability.
topics.thesisFraming: Spoilers for new releases should expire after one week of public availability. After one full week of release, spoilers should be fair game in public conversation.
counterClaims: Spoilers should not expire after just one week of public release.
Treating spoilers as expired after one week punishes anyone who waits to watch a new release.

evidenceFacts.label: Streaming release windows
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Streaming releases often see most viewership concentrated in the first 7 to 14 days.

evidenceFacts.label: Audience expectation surveys
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Surveys on spoiler etiquette show wide disagreement on appropriate spoiler-free windows after release.

tangentHooks: Album sequencing still matters in the playlist era — separate thread.

topics.title: Album sequencing still matters in the playlist era.
topics.resolution: Album sequencing still matters in the playlist era.
topics.thesisFraming: Album sequencing still matters in the playlist era. Good album sequencing gives listeners a built-in arc and a clear listening contract that a playlist can not replicate.
counterClaims: Album sequencing barely matters in the playlist era anymore.
Treating album sequencing as still important ignores how casual listeners use playlists.

evidenceFacts.label: Streaming listener behavior
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Streaming platform data shows casual users frequently shuffle albums rather than play them in sequence.

evidenceFacts.label: Critic-favorite album orderings
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Several critically acclaimed recent albums explicitly reference their intended sequencing in press notes.

tangentHooks: A mediocre sequel can improve the original — separate thread.

topics.title: A mediocre sequel can improve the original.
topics.resolution: A mediocre sequel can improve the original by clarifying its choices.
topics.thesisFraming: A mediocre sequel can improve the original by clarifying its choices. The mediocre sequel often makes the original sequel-worthy choices visible by failing to repeat them.
counterClaims: A mediocre sequel mainly drags the original down, not improves it.
Saying a mediocre sequel improves the original confuses contrast with quality.

evidenceFacts.label: Sequel reception analysis
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Critic round-ups frequently note that weaker sequels reframe original choices in new and clarifying ways.

evidenceFacts.label: Director commentary patterns
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Director commentaries on sequel releases often spend significant time defending original creative choices.

tangentHooks: Movie trailers should not show third-act footage — separate thread.

topics.title: Coffee shops should have quiet zones.
topics.resolution: Coffee shops should have quiet zones like libraries.
topics.thesisFraming: Coffee shops should have quiet zones like libraries. Quiet zones in coffee shops would protect focused work without banning conversation entirely.
counterClaims: Coffee shops are not libraries and should not enforce quiet zones.
Adding quiet zones to coffee shops will alienate the regulars who power the shops.

evidenceFacts.label: Coworking shop case studies
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Several coworking-style cafes report higher remote-work patron retention after adding quiet sections.

evidenceFacts.label: Library quiet-zone models
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Library quiet-zone policies are routinely cited in workspace design literature.

tangentHooks: Group chats are bad project management — separate thread.

topics.title: Group chats are bad project management.
topics.resolution: Group chats are bad project management compared to shared documents.
topics.thesisFraming: Group chats are bad project management compared to shared documents. Group chats are linear, ephemeral, and project decisions disappear into chat scrollback.
counterClaims: Group chats are an effective fast layer for project management decisions.
Calling group chats bad project management ignores how fast they unblock teams.

evidenceFacts.label: Team retro patterns
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Engineering retros frequently cite chat decisions that nobody can re-find as a recurring failure mode.

evidenceFacts.label: Document-first vs chat-first
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Process-comparison studies favor document-first decision logs for project clarity and retention.

tangentHooks: Coffee shops should have quiet zones — separate thread.

topics.title: Menus over 20 items are a red flag.
topics.resolution: Restaurant menus with more than 20 items are a red flag.
topics.thesisFraming: Restaurant menus with more than 20 items are a red flag. A menu with more than 20 items signals the kitchen is not focused, and the food usually reflects that.
counterClaims: Big menus with more than 20 items are not actually a red flag for the kitchen.
Treating menus over 20 items as a red flag ignores neighborhood diner culture.

evidenceFacts.label: Restaurant industry analysis
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Restaurant consultants frequently advise menu trimming as a quality and cost-control measure.

evidenceFacts.label: Neighborhood diner counterexamples
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Long-standing neighborhood diners often run menus well over 20 items with stable repeat-customer bases.

tangentHooks: Shared docs beat meetings 80% of the time — separate thread.

topics.title: A shared doc beats a meeting 80% of the time.
topics.resolution: A shared document beats a meeting 80% of the time.
topics.thesisFraming: A shared document beats a meeting 80% of the time. A well-structured shared document captures decisions, branches, and references better than a meeting captures the same content.
counterClaims: Shared documents do not beat meetings 80% of the time for actual decision making.
Calling a shared doc 80% better than a meeting underweights real-time team alignment.

evidenceFacts.label: Remote team retros
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Remote team retrospectives frequently identify meeting overuse as a top productivity drain.

evidenceFacts.label: Decision log studies
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Studies of high-output engineering teams favor written decision logs over meeting-only deciders.

tangentHooks: Group chats are bad project management — separate thread.

topics.title: Council meetings should enforce a 3-minute timer.
topics.resolution: City council meetings should enforce a strict three-minute public comment timer.
topics.thesisFraming: City council meetings should enforce a strict three-minute public comment timer. A strict three-minute timer in city council meetings keeps public comment fair and prevents agenda capture.
counterClaims: A strict three-minute timer in city council meetings cuts off complex public comment.
Forcing three-minute caps in council meetings punishes residents with detailed input.

evidenceFacts.label: Open-meeting research
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Open-meeting research notes shorter comment windows reduce monopolization by frequent commenters.

evidenceFacts.label: Council meeting case studies
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Several cities documented improved public-comment diversity after adopting fixed comment timers.

tangentHooks: Protected bike lanes are better than parking — separate thread.

topics.title: Protected bike lanes beat parking for curb space.
topics.resolution: Protected bike lanes are a better use of curb space than parking.
topics.thesisFraming: Protected bike lanes are a better use of curb space than parking. Protected bike lanes move more people per linear curb foot than parking does and reduce conflict points.
counterClaims: Protected bike lanes do not actually outperform parking for most curb users.
Treating bike lanes as better than parking ignores businesses that need curb loading.

evidenceFacts.label: Curb productivity studies
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Curb productivity research consistently finds protected bike lanes carry more daily users per foot than parking.

evidenceFacts.label: Business-impact research
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Multiple corridor studies show retail revenue stable or up after replacing parking with protected bike lanes.

tangentHooks: Libraries should host more after-hours events — separate thread.

topics.title: Libraries should host more after-hours community events.
topics.resolution: Libraries should host more after-hours community events.
topics.thesisFraming: Libraries should host more after-hours community events. After-hours library events serve community members who can not visit during weekday library hours.
counterClaims: Libraries should not be expected to host more after-hours community events without funding.
Pushing libraries into more after-hours community events strains library staff.

evidenceFacts.label: Library system reports
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Library system annual reports cite community programming as their fastest-growing service category.

evidenceFacts.label: Staffing strain surveys
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Library staff survey results frequently flag evening programming hours as a top burnout factor.

tangentHooks: Public comment should require a clear claim — separate thread.

topics.title: Public comment should require a clear claim.
topics.resolution: Public comment should require a clear claim and one supporting reason.
topics.thesisFraming: Public comment should require a clear claim and one supporting reason. Requiring a clear claim plus one reason in public comment keeps council deliberation legible and fair.
counterClaims: Requiring a clear claim and one reason in public comment is gatekeeping civic input.
Adding a clear-claim requirement to public comment will reduce participation by ordinary residents.

evidenceFacts.label: Civic input design research
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Civic input design research often advocates structured public-comment formats for council readability.

evidenceFacts.label: Pilot program outcomes
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Pilot programs with structured comment formats report higher decision-relevance ratings from council members.

tangentHooks: Three-minute council comment timer — separate thread.

topics.title: Read receipts make conversations worse.
topics.resolution: Read receipts make conversations worse on average.
topics.thesisFraming: Read receipts make conversations worse on average. Read receipts in conversations create response anxiety and pressure that hurts ordinary conversation flow.
counterClaims: Read receipts do not make conversations worse for most users.
Treating read receipts as bad for conversations ignores how they help close loops.

evidenceFacts.label: Messaging UX research
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Messaging UX research notes read-receipt anxiety as a common reason users disable the feature.

evidenceFacts.label: Workplace messaging studies
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Workplace messaging studies cite read receipts as a top driver of after-hours response pressure.

tangentHooks: Voice notes are hostile to searchable memory — separate thread.

topics.title: Autocorrect creates more arguments than it solves.
topics.resolution: Autocorrect creates more arguments than it solves in daily messaging.
topics.thesisFraming: Autocorrect creates more arguments than it solves in daily messaging. Autocorrect mistakes in daily messaging routinely trigger misread tone and small arguments.
counterClaims: Autocorrect prevents more daily messaging mistakes than it creates arguments.
Blaming autocorrect for arguments ignores how often it silently saves users from typos.

evidenceFacts.label: Autocorrect satisfaction surveys
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Autocorrect satisfaction surveys consistently show users perceive it as both helpful and annoying.

evidenceFacts.label: Linguistic miscommunication studies
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Studies of text-based miscommunication frequently cite autocorrect substitutions among top friction sources.

tangentHooks: Read receipts make conversations worse — separate thread.

topics.title: Voice notes are hostile to searchable memory.
topics.resolution: Voice notes are hostile to searchable memory in shared messaging.
topics.thesisFraming: Voice notes are hostile to searchable memory in shared messaging. Voice notes in shared messaging bury decisions, references, and context behind audio that no one can search later.
counterClaims: Voice notes are not actually hostile to searchable memory for most teams.
Calling voice notes hostile ignores how often they preserve tone better than text.

evidenceFacts.label: Team archive audits
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Team archive audits frequently find voice notes account for un-findable past decisions.

evidenceFacts.label: Voice-to-text transcript quality
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Automatic voice-to-text transcripts in messaging products still misrender technical terms at high rates.

tangentHooks: Every app should have a boring mode — separate thread.

topics.title: Every app should have a boring mode.
topics.resolution: Every consumer app should ship a boring mode that suppresses gamification.
topics.thesisFraming: Every consumer app should ship a boring mode that suppresses gamification. A boring mode in every consumer app would let users opt out of streaks, confetti, and gamification pressure.
counterClaims: Every consumer app does not need a boring mode opt-out for gamification.
Adding a boring mode in every consumer app would gut the engagement that funds the product.

evidenceFacts.label: App fatigue research
evidenceFacts.sourceText: App fatigue research notes streaks and notifications as top reasons users uninstall.

evidenceFacts.label: Reduced-motion settings adoption
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Reduced-motion settings adoption suggests significant user demand for app calm-down modes.

tangentHooks: Autocorrect creates more arguments than it solves — separate thread.

topics.title: Fries are a condiment delivery system.
topics.resolution: Fries are best understood as a condiment delivery system.
topics.thesisFraming: Fries are best understood as a condiment delivery system. Fries function as the medium for condiments and only secondarily as the food being delivered.
counterClaims: Fries are food, not a condiment delivery system.
Treating fries as a condiment delivery system insults fry quality.

evidenceFacts.label: Condiment-to-fry ratio menus
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Many fast-casual menus advertise fries by their sauce pairings rather than their fry style.

evidenceFacts.label: Bistro fry presentation
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Bistro fry presentation often emphasizes dipping sauces as much as the fries themselves.

tangentHooks: Tacos are structurally superior to sandwiches — separate thread.

topics.title: Brunch is mostly a scheduling problem.
topics.resolution: Brunch is mostly a scheduling problem, not a food category.
topics.thesisFraming: Brunch is mostly a scheduling problem, not a food category. Brunch as a scheduling problem explains why brunch food is so often mediocre but the brunch hangout still works.
counterClaims: Brunch is a real food category, not just a scheduling problem.
Calling brunch a scheduling problem dismisses how distinct brunch dishes are.

evidenceFacts.label: Brunch menu overlap analysis
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Brunch menu overlap analysis finds most weekend brunch menus repeat the same handful of dishes.

evidenceFacts.label: Weekend social schedule research
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Weekend social schedule research highlights brunch as a default 'meet between obligations' anchor.

tangentHooks: Tiny menus usually beat giant menus — separate thread.

topics.title: Tacos are structurally superior to sandwiches.
topics.resolution: Tacos are structurally superior to sandwiches as a portable food.
topics.thesisFraming: Tacos are structurally superior to sandwiches as a portable food. The taco's tortilla manages moisture, heat, and contents better than the sandwich's bread for portable meals.
counterClaims: Tacos are not structurally superior to sandwiches for most eaters.
Calling tacos structurally superior to sandwiches ignores how messy tacos actually are.

evidenceFacts.label: Portable food handling studies
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Portable food handling studies note taco fillings escape less than equivalent sandwich fillings.

evidenceFacts.label: Foodservice operator reviews
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Foodservice operator reviews often cite tacos as having higher per-unit margin than sandwiches at comparable price points.

tangentHooks: Fries are a condiment delivery system — separate thread.

topics.title: Tiny menus usually beat giant menus.
topics.resolution: Tiny menus usually beat giant menus on food quality.
topics.thesisFraming: Tiny menus usually beat giant menus on food quality. Tiny menus force the kitchen to refine each dish on the menu in a way giant menus never need to.
counterClaims: Tiny menus do not usually beat giant menus on food quality.
Treating tiny menus as quality proof ignores how giant menus serve real demand.

evidenceFacts.label: Restaurant operations research
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Restaurant operations research suggests smaller menus correlate with higher consistency scores.

evidenceFacts.label: Diner counterexamples
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Diner counterexamples show very large menus run by experienced kitchens can also achieve consistency.

tangentHooks: Brunch is mostly a scheduling problem — separate thread.

topics.title: Tabs are overused in product UI.
topics.resolution: Tabs are overused as a product UI pattern.
topics.thesisFraming: Tabs are overused as a product UI pattern. Tabs in product UI bury content and switch context in a way users dislike when overused.
counterClaims: Tabs are not actually overused in product UI for most workflows.
Calling tabs overused in product UI ignores how clearly they group content for users.

evidenceFacts.label: UX heuristic critiques
evidenceFacts.sourceText: UX heuristic critiques routinely list deeply nested tabs as a common product UI antipattern.

evidenceFacts.label: Hidden-content discoverability
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Discoverability studies find users miss content placed behind secondary tabs in product UI.

tangentHooks: Settings pages should have search by default — separate thread.

topics.title: Settings pages should have search by default.
topics.resolution: Settings pages should have search by default in modern product UI.
topics.thesisFraming: Settings pages should have search by default in modern product UI. Search by default in settings pages turns a sprawling product UI into a usable settings surface.
counterClaims: Settings pages do not need search by default for most users.
Adding search by default to settings pages encourages product teams to bloat settings.

evidenceFacts.label: Settings discoverability research
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Settings discoverability research consistently favors search alongside categorical navigation.

evidenceFacts.label: Heavy-settings app patterns
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Heavy-settings apps that introduced search reduced settings support tickets in published case studies.

tangentHooks: Tabs are overused — separate thread.

topics.title: Onboarding screens are usually an apology for bad UI.
topics.resolution: Long onboarding screens are usually an apology for bad UI.
topics.thesisFraming: Long onboarding screens are usually an apology for bad UI. Long onboarding screens patch over the bad UI that the product team did not fix at the core.
counterClaims: Long onboarding screens are not usually an apology for bad UI.
Calling long onboarding screens an apology underestimates the value of guided first sessions.

evidenceFacts.label: Onboarding drop-off studies
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Onboarding drop-off studies consistently find longer flows correlate with higher early-funnel drop.

evidenceFacts.label: Activation metric analysis
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Activation metric analysis often shows users who skip long onboarding still activate at similar rates.

tangentHooks: A useful empty state beats a tutorial — separate thread.

topics.title: A useful empty state beats a tutorial.
topics.resolution: A useful empty state beats a tutorial for first-time product users.
topics.thesisFraming: A useful empty state beats a tutorial for first-time product users. A useful empty state lets first-time product users see the shape of the product without a separate tutorial.
counterClaims: A useful empty state does not always beat a tutorial for first-time product users.
Treating empty states as better than tutorials assumes users will explore on their own.

evidenceFacts.label: First-session task completion data
evidenceFacts.sourceText: First-session task completion data favors actionable empty states over isolated tutorial walkthroughs.

evidenceFacts.label: Tutorial skip rates
evidenceFacts.sourceText: Tutorial skip rates in mature consumer products often exceed 50% on first launch.

tangentHooks: Onboarding screens are usually an apology — separate thread.
